nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zones of Lake Malawi; inhabits sediment-free rock piles and rubble-strewn areas along the shoreline, particularly around Nkhata Bay and the eastern shoreline

decorPreferences: large piles of stacked rocks forming caves and crevices
crushed coral or aragonite sand substrate to buffer pH
minimal planting (cichlids will uproot plants; optional tough species like Vallisneria or Java fern on rocks)
clear sight-line breaks between rock structures to reduce territorial conflict

diet.notes: In its natural habitat, the Socolofi Cichlid feeds on aufwuchs — the algae, biofilm, and tiny invertebrates that grow on and between rocky surfaces. In the aquarium it accepts high-quality spirulina-based flakes, algae wafers, cichlid pellets, and other vegetable-based prepared foods. Supplement occasionally with frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp and daphnia. Avoid protein-rich mammalian meats such as beef heart, which can trigger Malawi Bloat, a serious digestive condition. A high-fiber, plant-based diet is strongly recommended for long-term health.

goodWith: other similarly sized Lake Malawi mbuna cichlids (Melanochromis, Labidochromis, Iodotropheus)
robust Lake Malawi haplochromines of similar temperament
Synodontis catfish (Lake Tanganyika catfish commonly co-housed in Malawi community setups)
avoidWith: conspecific males (intense intermale aggression; keep only one male unless tank is very large)
passive or slow-moving fish that cannot escape harassment
small tetras and other soft-water community fish
Lake Victoria cichlids with similar blue coloration (mistaken-identity aggression)
shrimp and small invertebrates (will be eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen showing characteristic uniform powder-blue coloration. Verify current URL availability at FishBase summary page.

summary: The Socolofi Cichlid (Pseudotropheus socolofi) is a Lake Malawi mbuna — a rock-dwelling cichlid — notable for its uniform powder-blue coloration in both sexes, which is uncommon among mbuna. A maternal mouthbrooder reaching around 12 cm, it needs hard alkaline water, extensive rockwork, and a harem-style group with one male to several females.
careNotes: The Socolofi Cichlid is an endemic mbuna from the rocky shores of Lake Malawi. The species is also known under the more recent scientific name Chindongo socolofi following a 2016 taxonomic revision, though Pseudotropheus socolofi remains the name most widely used in the aquarium hobby. It is one of the few mbuna in which both sexes share the same striking powder-blue coloration, making sex determination difficult without close inspection of the anal fin — males typically display more prominent egg-spots on that fin than females. Maintain hard, alkaline water closely mimicking Lake Malawi chemistry: pH 7.8–8.6, GH 10–25, KH 10–20, and temperature 24–28°C. A crushed-coral or aragonite substrate helps buffer pH and maintain the necessary hardness. Tank décor should consist of extensive rockwork providing numerous caves and crevices; this is essential for reducing territorial aggression by allowing subordinate fish to escape dominant individuals’ line of sight. A minimum 200 L tank with a 120 cm footprint is recommended; larger is strongly preferred. The typical stocking approach is one male with three or more females. Intermale aggression tends to be intense — two males in the same tank will often result in one being killed or severely stressed unless the setup is very large with ample broken sightlines. Malawi Bloat, a digestive condition linked to high-protein animal-based foods, is a significant risk; a spirulina-heavy diet is strongly recommended. Water changes of 25–30% weekly are advised given the fish’s sensitivity to nitrate accumulation.
breedingNotes: The Socolofi Cichlid is a maternal mouthbrooder. The male displays to the female and spawning takes place on a flat rock surface; the female then collects the eggs in her mouth. During this process she is drawn toward the egg-spots on the male’s anal fin — markings that mimic eggs — which triggers sperm release and fertilizes the clutch while she holds it. The female broods the eggs and fry in her mouth for approximately 3–4 weeks, during which time she does not eat. Brood sizes are typically 15–30 fry. Upon release, the fry are fully formed and large enough to accept crushed cichlid flake and baby brine shrimp immediately. The female may hold multiple broods and tends to be stressed if not given an undisturbed refuge; a separate breeding or holding tank is advisable. Males tend to become aggressive toward brooding females, so providing dense rockwork or temporarily moving the female to a separate space during the brooding period is strongly recommended.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudotropheus socolofi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy, native range (Nkhata Bay and surrounding rocky coast, Lake Malawi), adult size (~10 cm TL), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.8, hardness 10–20 dH, temperature 24–28°C), diet (aufwuchs/spirulina-based), tank requirements, and breeding notes on maternal mouthbrooding. Reclassification to Chindongo socolofi (Li et al. 2016) noted.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudotropheus socolofi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 12.0 cm TL), native range (Lake Malawi, Malawi and Mozambique), temperature range (24–28°C), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), and diet (invertebrates and algae). Confirmed maternal mouthbrooder status and sexual monomorphism notes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Mbuna Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General Lake Malawi mbuna husbandry guidance used to cross-check tank sizing (75+ gallon / ~280 L recommended for mbuna community), diet (spirulina-based, avoid high-protein meats), water parameters, and stocking rationale (1 male : 3+ females). Socolofi not covered species-specifically.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Lake Malawi Mbuna
additional.notes: Supplementary source for mbuna aggression management, rockwork decor requirements, water chemistry guidance for Lake Malawi biotopes, and Malawi Bloat risk from protein-heavy feeding. Powder Blue Cichlid coloration and sexual monomorphism noted in mbuna overview context.

additional.name: Li, Z., et al. (2016) — Phylogenomics of Lake Malawi cichlids reclassification
additional.notes: Reference for the 2016 taxonomic revision by Li et al. reclassifying Pseudotropheus socolofi to Chindongo socolofi based on phylogenomic analysis. The Pseudotropheus name remains in widespread hobby use. FishBase nomenclature page used as a proxy accessible citation for this reclassification.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; broods 15–30 fry over ~3–4 weeks without feeding. Separate brooding female to reduce harassment from males. Fry accept crushed flake and baby brine shrimp immediately upon release.
